elements.sent-2411-001: This research investigates a non-extreme dyonic-like dilatonic charged black hole solution within a four-dimensional gravity model.
elements.sent-2411-002: This model incorporates two scalar (dilaton) fields and two Abelian vector fields, with interactions between the scalar and vector fields mediated by exponential terms involving two dilatonic coupling vectors.
elements.sent-2411-003: The solution is characterized by a dimensionless parameter $a$ (where $0 < a < 2$), which is specifically defined as a function of the dilatonic coupling vectors.
elements.sent-2411-004: The paper further explores solutions for timelike and null circular geodesics, which are crucial for understanding various astrophysical scenarios, including the quasinormal modes of different test fields in the eikonal approximation.
elements.sent-2411-005: For all values of $a$ the innermost stable circular orbit (ISCO) are found by means of reducing the problem to the solution of fourth order polynomial equation.
elements.sent-2411-006: The detection of gravitational waves in 2016 \cite{2016PhRvL.116f1102A sparked a surge of interest in the physics of stellar-mass black holes (BHs).}
elements.sent-2411-007: Einstein's century-old prediction of these ripples in spacetime led to an ambitious search, culminating in the construction of massive, exquisitely sensitive laser interferometers like LIGO, VIRGO, and others.
elements.sent-2411-008: These instruments, paired with advanced detection and data analysis techniques \cite{2016PhRvL.116v1101A , have opened a new window into the universe, allowing us to explore the most extreme phenomena in nature.}
elements.sent-2411-009: The heart of our Milky Way galaxy harbors a supermassive black hole (BH), a fact confirmed by meticulous observations and studies of stellar motion in this region \cite{1998ApJ...509..678G, 2000Natur.407..349G,2018A A...615L..15G .}
elements.sent-2411-010: These surveys, revealing the dynamics of star clusters surrounding the galactic center and the BH's mass, provide invaluable data for testing the predictions of general relativity (GR).
elements.sent-2411-011: By carefully tracking the movement of stars in this extreme environment, scientists can probe the fabric of spacetime and refine our understanding of gravity's impact on the universe.
elements.sent-2411-012: Capturing the elusive shadows of supermassive BHs at the heart of the M87 galaxy and our own Milky Way has been a monumental achievement, requiring an unprecedented level of scientific ingenuity and collaboration across the globe \cite{2019ApJ...875L...1E, 2022ApJ...930L..12E .}
elements.sent-2411-013: To achieve this, researchers effectively transformed our planet into a colossal radio telescope, coordinating observations from disparate groups of telescopes spanning all continents.
elements.sent-2411-014: The emergence of these BH shadows, along with their detailed analysis, provides compelling confirmation of the validity and power of GR.
elements.sent-2411-015: This remarkable achievement, coupled with decades of groundbreaking discoveries, underscores the paramount importance and enduring relevance of research in this field.
elements.sent-2411-016: While GR reigns supreme in our current understanding of gravity, alternative theories abound, offering modified and extended descriptions of gravitational phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-017: These alternative frameworks often predict BHs with additional parameters, distinct from the familiar Schwarzschild, Reissner-Nordstr\"{o m, and Kerr solutions \cite{Sotiriou:2006hs,2012PhR...513....1C,Astashenok:2014nua,Astashenok:2013vza,Astashenok:2017dpo,Capozziello:2019cav,Astashenok:2020qds .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, current observational capabilities, with their inherent error margins, limit our ability to definitively distinguish these exotic BHs from their GR counterparts \cite{2022ApJ...930L..17E .}
elements.sent-2411-019: As our observational precision improves, we may be able to unravel the true nature of these celestial objects and definitively test the validity of different gravitational theories.
elements.sent-2411-020: Observational data play a crucial role in constraining the parameters of BHs within various theoretical frameworks, including modified and extended theories of gravity \cite{2009PhRvD..79h4031P, 2022GReGr..54...44S, 2023Univ....9..147A,2023CQGra..40p5007V .}
elements.sent-2411-021: This wealth of observational information allows us to explore the properties of dyonic-like BHs with ``colored charges'' in the presence of scalar fields.
elements.sent-2411-022: This research delves into both the classic Schwarzschild and Reissner-Nordström BHs, as well as a variety of dilatonic BH models, each characterized by two distinct colored charges: both electric and magnetic ones.
elements.sent-2411-023: Through careful analysis of these diverse BH configurations, we aim to shed light on the intricate interplay between gravity, scalar fields, and the fundamental nature of these conjectural celestial objects.
elements.sent-2411-024: The dilaton, a hypothetical scalar field particle, interacts with the fabric of spacetime through coupling constants, which shape the metric and define a specific subclass of BHs.
elements.sent-2411-025: In certain limiting cases, these dilaton solutions smoothly transition to the well-known Schwarzschild and Reissner-Nordstr\"{o m solutions, their behavior determined by the specific values of these coupling constants.}
elements.sent-2411-026: While the dilaton is still elusive, its potential impact on the properties of BHs makes it an intriguing subject of research.
elements.sent-2411-027: Exploring the effects associated with its existence could reveal new insights into the complex interaction between gravity, scalar fields, and the nature of BHs themselves.
elements.sent-2411-028: This paper considers the intriguing question of whether we can differentiate between ordinary astrophysical BHs and their dilatonic counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our primary goal is to analyze the motion of neutral test particles and photons in circular orbits around these celestial objects, focusing on BHs with variety of ``color charges’’ (electric and magnetic ones) and coupling vectors.
elements.sent-2411-030: By scrutinizing the subtle differences in these orbital dynamics, we aim to reveal potential observational signatures that could distinguish dyonic-like dilatonic BHs from their more conventional counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-031: This investigation holds the potential to shed light on the mysterious properties of the dilaton and its impact.
elements.sent-2411-032: To achieve our goal of distinguishing between ordinary and dilatonic BHs, we embark on a comprehensive analysis of orbital dynamics, tackling the following key problems: - By applying the powerful framework of the Lagrange formalism, we derive the equations that govern the motion of test particles and photons in the spacetime warped by dilatonic BHs.
elements.sent-2411-033: This includes determining the radii of the innermost stable circular orbits (ISCO), a key parameter that marks the boundary between stable and unstable orbits.
elements.sent-2411-034: The intriguing properties of dilatonic dyonic BHs, characterized by both electric and magnetic charges and certain coupling vectors, have been extensively studied in recent years \cite{2015CQGra..32p5010A,ABI,2020JPhCS1690a2143B,MBI .}
elements.sent-2411-035: These investigations, utilizing a canonical scalar field, have yielded solutions by solving two master equations for the moduli functions.
elements.sent-2411-036: This analysis has revealed key physical parameters of these BHs, including their gravitational mass, scalar charge, Hawking temperature, area entropy, and parameterized post-Newtonian (PPN) parameters.
elements.sent-2411-037: While early research primarily focused on the intrinsic characteristics of these BHs \cite{2015CQGra..32p5010A,ABI,2020JPhCS1690a2143B , a subsequent study of Ref. ~\cite{MBI ventured into the realm of quasinormal modes, exploring the behavior of a massless test scalar field in the gravitational field surrounding a non-extremal dilatonic dyonic-like BH.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: This exploration opens new avenues for understanding the dynamics and potential observational signatures of these fascinating hypothetical celestial objects.
elements.sent-2411-039: The study of timelike and null geodesics, including circular orbits, is fundamental to understanding a wide range of astrophysical phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-040: These trajectories play critical roles in the dynamics of accretion disks, the generation of quasiperiodic oscillations, the behavior of quasinormal modes of test fields in the eikonal approximation \cite{2019PhRvD..99l4042K , and the formation of shadows cast by supermassive BHs \cite{2023PDU....4001178U,2023arXiv230400183L .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: By carefully analyzing these geodesics, researchers aim to discern potential differences between ordinary BHs and their dilatonic counterparts \cite{2009PhRvD..79h4031P , ultimately contributing to our understanding of these enigmatic objects and the fundamental nature of gravity.}
elements.sent-2411-042: This work breaks new ground by exploring the intricate paths of test particles in the gravitational field of dilatonic BHs with a unique twist: the inclusion of two scalar fields and color electric and magnetic charges.
elements.sent-2411-043: This marks the first study of its kind, venturing into a realm previously unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-044: While previous research has delved into the dynamics of geodesics within the contexts of standard BHs, naked singularities, and spinning deformed relativistic compact objects \cite{2021PhRvD.104h4009B,2016PhRvD..93b4024B,2013NCimC..36S..31B,2016IJMPA..3141006B , this investigation focuses specifically on the unique characteristics of dyonic-like dilatonic BHs with their complex interplay of scalar fields and color charges.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Complementing this research, the geometric and thermodynamic properties of dilatonic BHs have been explored in the presence of linear and nonlinear electromagnetic fields \cite{2018PhRvD..98h4006P,2017EPJC...77..647H,2017PhLB..767..214H,2016EPJC...76..296H,2015PhRvD..92f4028H .}
elements.sent-2411-046: This collaborative effort promises to deepen our understanding of these fascinating objects and the fundamental nature of gravity.
elements.sent-2411-047: A recent study in Ref.~\cite{CGP , explored a fascinating realm of closed photon orbits within spherically symmetric static solutions of various gravity theories, including supergravity, Horndeski theory, and quintessence.}
elements.sent-2411-048: These orbits, when unstable, trace a path known as a photon sphere (or an anti-photon sphere if stable).
elements.sent-2411-049: The study revealed a remarkable consistency: in all asymptotically flat solutions examined, those possessing a regular event horizon and an energy-momentum tensor satisfying the strong energy condition, only a single photon sphere exists outside the event horizon.
elements.sent-2411-050: This finding sheds light on the universal nature of photon sphere formation in these gravity theories, highlighting the fundamental role of the strong energy condition in shaping the geometry of spacetime around compact objects.
elements.sent-2411-051: While recent studies have explored the intriguing features of dilatonic BHs ~\cite{2022PhRvD.105l4056H,2020EPJC...80..654H,2010JHEP...03..100C,2008PThPS.172..161C , others have focused on solutions to field equations that, in specific limits, describe ordinary BHs .~\cite{2022PhRvD.106h4041L,2022EPJC...82..771S,2022EPJP..137..222T .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: These investigations, including those examining geodesics in the vicinity of astrophysical BHs ~\cite{2006ChPhL..23.1648Z,2015SerAJ.190...41B, 2018arXiv180500295S,2023Symm...15..329B,2022PhRvD.105l4009H , provide valuable insights into the rich landscape of gravitational phenomena.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Additionally, a number of works have explored the motion of test particles and photons around static \cite{2006ChPhL..23.1648Z,2015SerAJ.190...41B, 2018arXiv180500295S,2023Symm...15..329B,2022PhRvD.105l4009H and rotating \cite{2016PhRvD..94b4010S,2015PhRvD..92j4027F dilatonic BHs within various theories of gravity.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: However, to the best of our knowledge, no previous studies, but that of Refs.
elements.sent-2411-055: This unexplored territory represents a significant frontier in understanding the complex interplay between these fields and their impact on the geometry of spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-056: In the realm of classical physics, gravity's influence is inextricably linked to mass, leaving charge unaffected.
elements.sent-2411-057: This separation of forces leads to a distinct lack of interplay between gravitational and electromagnetic fields.
elements.sent-2411-058: However, within the framework of GR, the picture becomes richer and more intricate.
elements.sent-2411-059: Gravity, in addition to its dependence on mass, can be generated by a rotating object's kinetic energy (the Lense-Thirring effect), the presence of electric or magnetic charges, the energy associated with their electromagnetic fields, and even other types of fields.
elements.sent-2411-060: This interconnectedness implies that gravity exerts its influence on the motion of both neutral and charged particles in GR.
elements.sent-2411-061: These effects become particularly pronounced in the intense gravitational fields surrounding BHs and neutron stars, where the curvature of spacetime becomes significant.
elements.sent-2411-062: Interestingly, neutral and charged particles exhibit distinct behavior under the influence of gravity.
elements.sent-2411-063: While neutral particles follow geodesic paths in the absence of external forces, charged particles can deviate significantly from these geodesic trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-064: The curvature of spacetime itself, generated by massive and compact objects, can dramatically alter the paths of charged particles.
elements.sent-2411-065: Moreover, the presence of strong magnetic fields introduces the Lorentz force, further deviating charged particles from geodesic motion.
elements.sent-2411-066: While magnetic fields can influence the motion of neutral test particles, this influence is typically less pronounced.
elements.sent-2411-067: Similarly, strong electric fields exert Coulomb forces on charged particles, leading to deviations from geodesic motion.
elements.sent-2411-068: This effect becomes particularly relevant in astrophysics, where charged particles navigate the intense magnetic and electric fields surrounding neutron stars or within astrophysical jets.
elements.sent-2411-069: A comprehensive overview of the interplay between rotation, the cosmological constant, and magnetic fields on the motion of charged and neutral particles within accretion disks surrounding Kerr BH candidates is presented in Ref.~\cite{2020Univ....6...26S .}
elements.sent-2411-070: This work explores realistic astrophysical scenarios around compact objects, encompassing the characteristics of thin and thick, neutral and charged accretion disks, quasiperiodic oscillations, and relativistic jets.
elements.sent-2411-071: This paper examines a specific solution for a dyonic-like dilatonic BH, previously derived in \cite{MBI , and deals with particular solutions for null and timelike geodesics within this spacetime.}
elements.sent-2411-072: The BH solution under consideration arises from a gravitational model featuring two ``neutral'' (real-valued) scalar fields and two Abelian gauge fields.
elements.sent-2411-073: The study of spherically symmetric solutions in gravitational models is a topic of ongoing interest, as exemplified by numerous investigations, including those found in Refs.~\cite{BS,1988NuPhB.298..741G,1991PhRvD..43.3140G,1992PhRvD..45.3888G and others.}
elements.sent-2411-074: These solutions often arise in models incorporating scalar fields and antisymmetric forms.
elements.sent-2411-075: In our analysis, we adopt a methodology similar to that employed in Ref. ~\cite{2011PhRvD..83b4021P , where the motion of neutral test particles in the gravitational field of a Reissner-Nordstr\"{o m BH was explored.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: This approach provides a solid foundation for investigating the dynamics of particles in more complex and intriguing spacetimes.
elements.sent-2411-077: This paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-078: In Section \ref{sec:metrics we introduce charged BH solutions characterized by two scalar (dilaton) fields and two Abelian vector fields.}
elements.sent-2411-079: The key features of these solutions are discussed in detail.
elements.sent-2411-080: Section \ref{sec:geod_motion focuses on the analysis of geodesics followed by neutral test particles and photons.}
elements.sent-2411-081: We derive expressions for energy, angular momentum, and effective potential.
elements.sent-2411-082: We also scrutinize their behavior and analyze the stability of circular geodesics.
elements.sent-2411-083: In Section \ref{sec:examples certain examples of solutions are explored.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Finally, in Section \ref{sec:conclusion , we summarize our results and discuss potential lines for future research.}

elements.sent-2411-086: Here, and in what follows, we set $G =c =1$ (where $G$ is the gravitational constant and $c$ is the speed of light in vacuum.) We consider a dyonic-like BH solution \cite{MBI to the field equations corresponding to the action (\ref{i.1 ) which is defined on the (oriented) manifold \beq{i.2 {\cal M = \R \times(2\mu, + \infty) \times S^2 , \eeq and has the following form \bear{i4.9 ds^2 = H^{a}\biggl\{-H^{-2a \left( 1 - \frac{2\mu {R \right) dt^2 \nonumber \color{white + \quad\qquad\qquad +\frac{dR^2 {1 - \frac{2\mu {R + R^2 d \Omega^2 \biggr\}, \varphi^i = \nu^i \ln H , \ear with the 2-form defined by \beq{i4.10em F^{(1) = \frac{Q_1 {H^2 R^2 dt \wedge dR, \quad F^{(2) = Q_2 \tau, \eeq where $Q_1$ is the (color) electric charge and $Q_2$ are the (color) magnetic charge.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Here the extremality parameter is denoted by $\mu > 0$, the metric on the unit sphere $S^2$ is given by $d \Omega^2 = d \theta^2 + \sin^2 \theta d \phi^2$, where $0 < \theta < \pi$ and $0 < \phi < 2 \pi$, and the standard volume form on $S^2$ is $\tau = \sin \theta d \theta \wedge d \phi$.
elements.sent-2411-088: The moduli function is defined as \beq{i4.7 H = 1 + \frac{P {R .}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: It can be readily verified that \beq{i.18BD \Delta > 0, \eeq is valid for $\vec{\lambda _1 \neq - \vec{\lambda _2$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: Indeed, in this case we have the sum of two non-negative terms in (\ref{i4.10n ): $\frac{1 {2 (\vec{\lambda _1 + \vec{\lambda _2)^2 > 0$ and \beq{i.18BC C = \vec{\lambda _1^2 \vec{\lambda _2^2 - (\vec{\lambda _1 \vec{\lambda _2)^2 \geq 0, \eeq because of the Cauchy--Schwarz inequality.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Here, $C = 0$ if and only if vectors $\vec{\lambda _1$ and $\vec{\lambda _2$ are collinear.}}
elements.sent-2411-093: We note that due to relations (\ref{i4.8bdd ) and (\ref{i.18BD ), the quantities of charges $Q_s$ are well defined (up to signs).}}
elements.sent-2411-094: The gravitational mass is given by a relation where parameters $\mu$, $a$ and $P$ are used ~\cite{MBI ,: \beq{i.18bb M = \mu + \frac{a {2 P.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Calculating the scalar curvature for the metric $ds^2 = g_{\mu \nu dx^{\mu dx^{\nu}$ from (\ref{i4.9 ) yields the following result \cite{MBI : \beq{i4.8R R[g] = \frac{a(2-a)P^2 (R - 2 \mu) {2 R^{4 - a (R+ P)^{1 + a .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Table~\ref{tab:amuP presents $\mu$ and $P$ in terms of $M$ and $Q$ for certain values of $a$.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \setlength{\tabcolsep}{1.em} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.1} \begin{tabular}{lcc} $a$ $\mu$ $P$ 0.00 $M$ -- 0.25 $\frac{7M}{6} - \frac{\sqrt{8M^2 + 3Q^2}}{12\sqrt{2}}$ $-\frac{4M}{3} + \frac{\sqrt{2}}{3}\sqrt{8M^2 + 3Q^2}$ 0.50 $\frac{3M}{2}-\frac{1}{4}\sqrt{4M^2+Q^2}$ $-2M+\sqrt{4M^2+Q^2}$ 0.75 $\frac{5M}{2} - \frac{3 \sqrt{8M^2 + Q^2}}{4\sqrt{2}}$ $-4M + \sqrt{2} \sqrt{8M^2 + Q^2}$ 1.00 $M-\frac{Q^2}{8M}$ $\frac{Q^2}{4M}$ 1.25 $-\frac{3M}{2} + \frac{5 \sqrt{8M^2 - Q^2}}{4\sqrt{2}}$ $4M - \sqrt{2} \sqrt{8M^2 - Q^2}$ 1.50 $-\frac{M}{2}+\frac{3}{4}\sqrt{4M^2-Q^2}$ $2M-\sqrt{4M^2-Q^2}$ 1.75 $ -\frac{M}{6} + \frac{7 \sqrt{8M^2 - 3Q^2}}{12\sqrt{2}}$ $\frac{4M}{3} - \frac{\sqrt{2} }{3}\sqrt{8M^2 - 3Q^2}$ 2.0 $\sqrt{M^2-\frac{Q^2}{2}}$ $M-\sqrt{M^2-\frac{Q^2}{2}}$ \end{tabular} \caption{Values of parameters $\mu$ and $P$ in terms of $M$ and $Q$ depending on $a$. Note that for vanishing $Q$ one obtains $P=0$ and $\mu=M$. At the same time, for vanishing $a$, one recovers the Schwarzschild metric, and $\mu=M$ and $P$ disappears in the metric} \label{tab:amuP}
elements.sent-2411-097: The study of geodesics is fundamental to understanding the motion of test particles within the gravitational field of dilatonic BHs.
elements.sent-2411-098: Such analysis offers valuable insight into the nature of these objects and their influence on the surrounding spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-099: These geodesics are derived from the Lagrangian: \be \mathcal{L = \frac{1 {2 g_{\alpha \beta (x) \dot{x ^{\alpha}\dot{x ^{\beta .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: The variable $\tau$ is affine parameter in the case of null geodesics and the proper time for a massive point-like particle which moves along timelike geodesics, respectively, $\alpha=0, 1, 2, 3$.
elements.sent-2411-101: The generalized momentum, $p_{\alpha = g_{\alpha \beta (x) \dot{x ^{\beta}$, derived from the Lagrangian ~\eqref{eq:Lag , is normalized as follows: \be g^{\alpha \beta (x) p_{\alpha p_{\beta = g_{\alpha \beta (x) u^{\alpha u^{\beta = -k.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: For a general value of $k$, the quantity $(-k/2)$ represents the conserved energy for the Lagrangian ~\eqref{eq:Lag .}
elements.sent-2411-103: For brevity, we will not consider spacelike geodesics in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-104: Without loss of generality we consider null and timelike geodesics confined to the equatorial plane ($\theta = \pi/2$).
elements.sent-2411-105: For the metric given by Eq. ~\eqref{i4.9 , the Lagrangian is: \be \mathcal{L = \frac{1 {2 H^a\left[-H^{-2a}\left(1-\frac{2\mu {R}\right)\dot{t ^2 + \frac{ \dot{R ^2 {1-\frac{2\mu {R + R^2 \dot{\phi ^2\right].}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: For the line element from Eq.~\eqref{i4.9 it reads as follows \be -H^{-a}\left(1-\frac{2\mu {R}\right)\dot{t ^2 + \frac{ H^a \dot{R ^2 {1-\frac{2\mu {R + H^a R^2 \dot{\phi ^2 = -k.. \ee Using Eqs.}}}}}}}}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Veff.pdf} \caption{The effective potential of test particles for $L/(\mu m)=0, 3, 4, 5, 6$ with different $a=0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, 1.5, 1.75, 2$ as a function of the dimensionless/normalized radial coordinate $R/(2\mu)$ \cite{BSIU}. As one may notice that for decreasing $L$, the minimum of the effective potential shifts from right to left. } \label{fig:Veff_pl}
elements.sent-2411-108: For $\dot{R \neq 0$, it follows just from Eq.~\eqref{eq:EqForR .}}
elements.sent-2411-109: For circular trajectory with $\dot{R = 0$, the radial equation ~\eqref{eq:EqForRtrue reads \be \frac{\partial V {\partial R = 0, \ee while equation (\ref{eq:EqForR ) takes the following form \be V^2 = \frac{E^2 {m^2 .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: We now focus on timelike geodesics $(k=1)$.
elements.sent-2411-111: In Figure \ref{fig:Veff_pl , we illustrate the behavior of the effective potential as a function of $R/\mu$ for a fixed value of $Q/\mu = 0.6$ (which is roughly $P/\mu = 0.0863$) and different values of $L/(\mu m)$.}
elements.sent-2411-112: The cases of $a = 0$, $a = 1$, and $a = 2$ formally correspond to the Schwarzschild, Sen \cite{1992PhRvL..69.1006S , and Reissner--Nordstr\"{o m solutions, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-113: It is clearly seen that the maximum of the effective potential for $a = 0$ exceeds the maximums for other cases of $a$.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{Visco.pdf} \caption{The effective potential of test particles with $L_{ISCO}$ for different $a$=0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, 1.5, 1.75, 2 as a function of the dimensionless (radial) coordinate $R/(2\mu)$ \cite{BSIU}. The dots represent the inflection points ($R_{ISCO}/(2\mu)$, $V_{ISCO} = V(R_{ISCO}/(2\mu))$)} \label{fig:Visco}
elements.sent-2411-114: In Fig.~\ref{fig:Visco , the effective potential at fixed $L =L_{ISCO}$ (see below) is illustrated as a function of the dimensionless radial coordinate $R/(2\mu)$ for different $a$=0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, 1.5, 1.75, 2.}
elements.sent-2411-115: Here dots show the inflection points.
elements.sent-2411-116: As shown, the locations of these points, expressed in terms of $R$ and $\mu$, differ from those of standard Schwarzschild and Reissner--Nordstr\"{o m BHs.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Here, we consider circular motions, which are described by condition: $\dot{R = 0$, so $V = E/m$.}
elements.sent-2411-118: The first derivative of the effective potential with respect to $R$ reads: \bea \frac{\partial V {\partial R = \left[2R^3 H^{2+a V\right]^{-1}\Big[H^a (a P(R-2\mu)\nonumber + 2\mu(P+R))+\frac{2L^2 {m^2 R^2}\Big\{R(3\mu-R)\nonumber + P(R(a-1)+\mu(3-2a))\Big\}\Big].}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: The distinctions between the curves for different values of $a$ are larger for larger values of $Q/\mu$.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{E_of_R.pdf} \caption{Energy $E/m$ of test particles as a function of $R/\mu$ for cases $a$=0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, 1.5, 1.75, 2, if $Q/\mu=0.6$ \cite{BSIU}. The dots represent the values of $R_{ISCO}/\mu$ and the corresponding $E_{ISCO}/\mu$.} \label{fig:E_of_R(a=0-2)}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{L_of_R.pdf} \caption{Orbital angular momentum $L/(\mu m)$ of test particles as a function of $R/\mu$ for cases $a$=0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, 1.5, 1.75, 2, if $Q/\mu=0.6$ \cite{BSIU}. elements.sent-2411-122: The innermost stable circular orbit (ISCO) is a crucial concept in the study of objects around BHs.
elements.sent-2411-123: It is particularly significant in the context of accretion disks, as it determines the inner edge of these spinning structures.
elements.sent-2411-124: Any object that enters the ISCO is inevitably pulled into the BH.
elements.sent-2411-125: To maintain their integrity, accretion disks must have a radius greater than the ISCO.
elements.sent-2411-126: The location of the ISCO, along with characteristics such as the size, temperature, and luminosity of the accretion disk, provides valuable insights into the behavior of BHs and their surrounding environment.
elements.sent-2411-127: It may be verified that the following is valid \begin{equation}\frac{\partial^2 V^2}{\partial R^2} = 2V \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial R^2} = \frac{(1-2\mu/R)(1+P/R)^{-a}}{\Delta_1} W. \label{d2VdR2}\end{equation} Here $\Delta_1 > 0$, since $\Delta > 0$, see Eq.~\eqref{eq:Delta_1 .}

elements.sent-2411-160: In this paper, we have explored the solution for a dyonic-like dilatonic BH.
elements.sent-2411-161: We have investigated circular geodesics of massive, neutral test particles and photons, by using various values for the metric parameters: $a$ , $\mu$ and $P$, which are related to the physical quantities: BH mass $M$ and the net charge $Q$.
elements.sent-2411-162: We have calculated $R_{ISCO}$, which is the radius of the innermost stable circular orbit for test particles in terms of these parameters.
elements.sent-2411-163: It satisfies the inequality $R_{ISCO > R_{0}$, where $R_0$ is the radius of photonic sphere.}
elements.sent-2411-164: The circular orbits of massive particles with radius $R$ which obey $R > R_{ISCO}$ are shown to be stable while those satisfying $R_0 < R < R_{ISCO}$ are shown to be unstable.
elements.sent-2411-165: For all values of $a$ ($0 < a < 2$) the radius of ISCO is found by means of reducing the problem to the solution of fourth order polynomial equation $F(x) = 0$ - the so-called master equation for a dimensionless parameter $x$.
elements.sent-2411-166: The solution to master equation $x = x_{isco}$ which corresponds to ISCO radius is presented in Appendix ($x_{isco = x_{4}$).}
elements.sent-2411-167: We have presented few explicit examples of solutions to master equation for $a= 0, 1/4, 1, 2$ which contain a new one for $a= 1/4$.
elements.sent-2411-168: Hopefully this second derivative has turned to be proportional to the fourth order polynomial $F(x)$ and this fact has drastically simplified our analysis of stability.
elements.sent-2411-169: Here we have also found an analytical relation for the efficiency of converting matter into radiation by using the solution to master equation $x_{isco = x_{4}$.}
elements.sent-2411-170: The numerical analysis of the efficiency tells us that the efficiency is larger for the case of Reissner--Nordstr\"{o m BH ($a = 2$) $\eta=8.14\%$ and smaller for the case of Schwarzschild one ($a = 0$) $\eta=5.72\%$ \cite{2020PhRvD.102l4078B .}}
elements.sent-2411-171: All other configurations lie within the parameter space bounded by these two cases.
elements.sent-2411-172: This peculiarity can be used to distinguish ordinary or astrophysical BHs from the dyonic-like dilatonic BHs.

elements.sent-2411-219: For convenience, we use different notations from the previous Appendix.
elements.sent-2411-220: So, here the master equation is given by: \begin{equation}A_4 x^4 + A_3 x^3 + A_2 x^2 + A_1 x + A_0 = 0 ,\end{equation} where the coefficients $A_i$ are \begin{eqnarray}A_0 = (a - 2)(a - 1)(2a - 3)p^3 , A_1 = (-18 + 25a - 9a^2)p^2 \nonumber + (2 - a)(1 - 7a + 4a^2)p^3 , A_2 = 6(2a - 3)p + 3(2 - 9a + 5a^2)p^2 , \nonumber + 2a(a - 1)(a - 2)p^3 A_3 = -6 + 6(1 - 2a)p + 6a(1 - a)p^2 , A_4 = 2(1 + ap).\end{eqnarray}.
elements.formula-2411-062: \begin{equation}A_4 x^4 + A_3 x^3 + A_2 x^2 + A_1 x + A_0 = 0 ,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-221: The solutions of the master equation are shown as \begin{eqnarray}x_1 = -\frac{A_3}{4A_4} - B_7 - B_8 , \label{B_x1} x_2 = -\frac{A_3}{4A_4} - B_7 + B_8 , \label{B_x2}\end{eqnarray} \begin{eqnarray}x_3 = -\frac{A_3}{4A_4} + B_7 - B_9 , \label{B_x3} x_4 = -\frac{A_3}{4A_4} + B_7 + B_9 . \label{B_x4}\end{eqnarray} Only $x_4$ corresponds to physical ISCO solution and other solutions are either essentially complex or negative or smaller than the dimensionless photon sphere radius.
elements.sent-2411-222: The remaining coefficients are listed as follows: \begin{eqnarray}B_9 = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{ B_2 - \frac{2^{1/3} B_0}{3 A_4 B_6} - \frac{B_6}{3 \cdot 2^{1/3} A_4} + \frac{B_3}{8 B_7}} , B_8 = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{ B_2 - \frac{2^{1/3} B_0}{3 A_4 B_6} - \frac{B_6}{3 \cdot 2^{1/3} A_4} - \frac{B_3}{8 B_7}} , B_7 = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{ \frac{B_2}{2} + \frac{2^{1/3} B_0}{3 A_4 B_6} + \frac{B_6}{3 \cdot 2^{1/3} A_4}} ,\end{eqnarray} \begin{eqnarray}B_6 = (B_4 + B_5)^{1/3} , B_5 = \sqrt{ B_4^2 - 4 B_0^3} , B_4 = B_1-27 A_0 A_3^2 + 72 A_0 A_2 A_4 + 54 A_0 A_4^2 B_2 ,\quad\end{eqnarray} \begin{eqnarray}B_3 = -\frac{A_3^3}{A_4^3} + \frac{4 A_2 A_3}{A_4^2} - \frac{8 A_1}{A_4} , B_2 = \frac{A_3^2}{2 A_4^2} - \frac{4 A_2}{3 A_4} , B_1 = 2 A_2^3 - 9 A_2 (A_1 A_3 + 8 A_0 A_4) \nonumber + 27 (A_0 A_3^2 + A_1^2 A_4) , B_0 = A_2^2 - 3 A_1 A_3 + 12 A_0 A_4 .\end{eqnarray}.
